title: Ronin Part One
original_prose:
he cell opens like it forgot it was a prison.

No alarm. No signal flare. Just the breath of power loss. A hiss behind the walls. The lights overhead jitter once, then fall into a stuttering loop—fluorescent seizures, fading propaganda.

I step out. Legs slow. Back straight.

It’s the same hall. Or maybe just a copy. Same ash-colored panels. Same vein of black glass cut down the spine. Same doors—some open, some welded shut.

Kat’s face flickers on every screen. Bound. Bloodied. Silent.

Then flame.

The loop cuts just before her final scream. Then restarts.

The building breathes around me. Not alive. Just decaying. Like it remembers being holy once, and can’t quite let it go.

I walk.

No guards. No alerts. Just the dead hum of a system dying out loud.

I pass through a long corridor that looks identical to where we first ran. Where she bled and smiled and threw me the rifle like it was a wedding ring.

It might be the same tower. Maybe not. There are so many of them.

The air smells like ozone and memory.

I'm not going down the meat chute, this time. I take the stairs. The lobby opens like a wound.

One man. SynRgy black. Low-rank. Still strapped into his chair like he didn’t believe the alarms. He stands when he sees me.

Too slow.

I move before I think. He doesn’t go for the sword. Just his sidearm.

I’m faster.

He gets the grip halfway out of the holster before I’m on him. My hand closes over his. One twist, one shove—his wrist shatters. He screams.

I take the pistol. Still warm from his palm.

One shot. Close enough to taste the carbon.

He drops. Dead or dying. Doesn’t matter.

I strip his belt. Mags, light, backup blade, sidearm. All of it. Strap it on like old skin.

The sword’s still humming. Low. Approving.

Now I’m armed.

Now it begins.

He draws. I’m closer.

Steel across the throat. One motion. No glory.

He falls without sound. Just a wet gasp and twitch.

I take his sidearm. Slap a new mag into the rig. Sheathe the blade.

The sword hums once.

I walk toward the exit.

I’m not here to win.

I’m here to finish it.



####

The street greets me like a war crime.

Vegas isn’t Vegas anymore. SynRgy One bleeds neon and smoke—signs shorting out, towers hemorrhaging flame. A billboard meant to show Voss’s face glitches into static, then a rebel flag, then a child screaming. The system’s dying, and it knows it.

Smoke and warning sirens curl through the skyline like dying nerves. Somewhere above, SynRgy dropships whine low across the horizon, searchlights cutting through ash and fog. Some of them make it to their targets. Some just burn midair and drop like bones from the gods.

I don’t flinch.

I move like a weapon with a deadline.

A SynRgy drone swoops low. Civilian scanner. Red light flashes.

I draw and fire once. The drone spirals into the side of a monorail car and explodes.

A woman screams nearby—then keeps running.

I round the corner and walk into a firefight. Rebels dug in behind a crushed delivery rig. SynRgy pushing forward—eight of them, shields up, pulse rifles barking.

I don’t pause. I flank wide, cut behind a half-collapsed souvenir stand. Pop up behind the last one in the squad.

The blade sings.

I take his spine. Drop him. Two more turn—one fires, misses. I shoot once. Throat. Then again. Eyeball.

Another lunges. I duck under his swing, slam the hilt of the sword into his knee. He goes down screaming. I end it clean.

The rebels hold.

One of them shouts something—maybe my name. I don’t stop. Don’t look back.

Next block. Smoke thicker. Sirens louder.

A SynRgy security mech crawls over the husk of a car, its arms like crab legs tipped in steel.

I drop behind a crushed lightpost, breathe once, then run.

Slide under its legs. Fire three shots straight into the central joint. It glitches, spasms, slams face-first into the pavement.

I drive the blade into the base of its spine—where the joints still twitch and the armor's softest. Sparks leap. The whole rig seizes, convulses once, then drops in a heap of metal and smoke.

Keep moving.

An alley. Quiet. For a breath.

Two SynRgy scouts step out from a side door, mid-laugh. Their eyes go wide.

They recognize me.

I don’t give them time to regret it.

One shot to the gut. He folds. The other fumbles for his blade. I kick it away and slam his head into the wall. Once. Twice. A third time.

He stops moving.

My hands are shaking. Not from fear.

From speed.

The sword is warm now. Not hot. Not humming. Just ready.

I climb a rubble slope into what used to be a plaza. There’s a makeshift SynRgy barricade—concrete, steel, two mounted turrets.

Five soldiers. Maybe six. A commander screaming orders.

I go low.

Throw a flash grenade I stole off the guard back in the lobby.

It bursts white. I move into it.

The first doesn’t see me. I take him at the hip, blade slicing through both legs. He collapses.

The second turns too slow. I take his rifle and shoot his squadmate in the back of the head.

Commander spins. Draws.

I throw the blade.

It sticks in his chest. He gurgles. Falls.

I pick it back up. Wipe it on his coat.

The plaza is clear.

And then—gunfire above. Screams.

I look up.

SynRgy tower ablaze. Its glass ribs snapping. Rebels on the upper decks, pushing through windows. One drops a makeshift explosive.

Boom.

Fire ripples down the side like oil.

Something in the street shifts.

A man in black armor lurches from the wreckage of a shuttle. WRAITH-pattern, but slow. Hesitating.

He sees me.

I raise the sword.

He charges.

I duck under the first swing. Catch his second arm with the edge of the blade—deep. Metal screams.

He kicks me. Hard. I roll, land in glass. Shoulder’s bleeding. Doesn’t matter.

He rushes again. I sidestep. Slash the back of his knee. He buckles. I bring the blade down across the back of his neck.

It takes two more hits to drop him.

I breathe. Look around.

The street is soaked.

The rebels are holding.

A group to my right—Red Rocks faces. I recognize them. One shouts my name.

I don’t look.

I don’t stop.

The sword hums. Louder this time.

It remembers.

So do I.



####

I round the next corner with the pistol raised.

He’s already there.

Gun drawn. Two steps away. Barrel aimed straight between my eyes.

I freeze. He doesn’t.

Bambi.

We stand there, breathing smoke, heat between us like a coiled spring. His eyes are full of fury. Mine, full of nothing left to lose.

“You motherfucker,” he says.

I say nothing.

“You said you wouldn’t hurt her.”

“I didn’t,” I say. Voice flat. “Voss did.”

“Same fuckin’ shit.”

The barrel of his rifle doesn’t waver. Neither does mine.

For a second, I think he’s going to pull the trigger. I want him to. It’d be simpler.

“Then shoot me,” I say. “Or move.”

Bambi stares me down like he’s weighing it. One twitch. One breath.

Then he exhales.

“Fuck,” he mutters. Shoulders sag just slightly.

He reaches into a pouch on his vest. Tosses something toward me. I catch it without lowering the weapon.

A USB key. Old. Scorched. Marked with rebel sigils.

“What is it?”

“The nerds say it’ll break his link to the WRAITHs.”

I look at it. Then back at him. “You want them free?”

“Fuck no. But we’re getting stomped. They’re stunned, not broken. We need something to tip it.”

I stare at him.

He nods toward the skyline. “We got people dying in that tower. You got a better idea?”

“No.”

We both lower our weapons.

“I still might kill you when this is over,” he says.

“Get in line.”

I turn without another word.

I don’t look back.

The key burns cold in my fist.

The sword hums like it knows the next part.



####

The terminal’s tucked into the bones of a collapsed kiosk—half-eaten by rust, cracked down the center like something punched it from inside.

I clear the debris with one boot. Kneel. Dust off the interface. It flickers to life without a password. SynRgy arrogance. Still thinks it owns the grid.

I plug in the key.

Nothing happens.

Then everything does.

The screen goes black.

Then white.

Then something else. Not code. Not UI. Something older. Symbols like bone etchings. Glyphs that hum in my jawbone. The air changes. Thicker. Charged.

A line of characters scrolls by, too fast to read. Then:

“RECLAMATION LINK BREACHED.” “VESSEL CHAINS REMOVED.” “YIELD UNCAPPED.”

A tone like teeth grinding in harmony buzzes out of the speakers. The screen dies.

I don’t move.

Somewhere above me, a scream. Then another. Then something worse: silence.

Then:

Gunfire. Shouting. A crash.

And then I see it.

A WRAITH lurches into view two streets over. Black armor. No insignia. No leash.

It pauses.

Looks around like it just woke up in the wrong body.

A SynRgy enforcer steps into view, shouting commands.

The WRAITH turns.

And it fucking leaps.

Crushes the enforcer’s skull like it was never meant to be solid. Turns to the next soldier. Doesn’t stop. Doesn’t hesitate.

It rips through them.

Effortless. Efficient. Free.

Two more WRAITHs round the corner. Pause. Scan.

Then join in.

No protocol. No orders.

Just rage unbound.

I don’t cheer.

I don’t blink.

I stand. Holster the pistol. Wipe my hand on my chest rig. Pick up the blade.

The sword hums so loud now it drowns the street.

I look toward the Stratosphere.

And start walking.



####

The climb starts in the ruins of Fremont. Or what used to be Fremont.

Ash falls like memory. WRAITHs tear through SynRgy squads on sight now—uncaring, uncoordinated. Just rage machines given permission. One tears a lieutenant in half across a synth-concrete wall as I pass. Doesn’t even look at me.

It’s not that they know me.

It’s that I’m already dead.

I cut through the corpse smoke and twisted barricades like a needle through scar tissue. Every corner holds a kill. Every corridor a test.

SynRgy loyalists form pockets—desperate. Back-to-wall screaming. They still think the Protocol will reboot. They still pray to the grid.

They don’t see me coming.

The first squad—six strong. One has a lancer rig. I drop the first with a headshot mid-sentence. Duck behind a rusted patrol drone. Bounce a frag. Two down.

One charges. Blade raised.

I let him get close.

Then I spin. Slide the sword across his hip. Watch him fold in half and die praying.

The lancer fires. Misses. Recoil throws him off-balance. I cross the space in three steps and take his hand before he can scream.

The other stays down. Smart. Or paralyzed.

I don’t check.

I reach the Strip. Or what’s left of it. Half the towers are slag. The other half are burning. Screens glitch above the streets like dying saints.

Kat’s image flashes again. Her body. The flames. Looping.

I don’t look away.

Another SynRgy squad ahead. They see me. Panic. Bad formation. One fires too soon. Bullet grazes my arm.

I take the corner fast. Bounce off a crumbling ATM. Fire three times without looking.

Hit twice. Third’s enough.

I leap the barrier. Sword drawn.

The last one begs.

I don’t speak. I don’t stop.

The blade takes his throat.

I enter what used to be the central square. It’s a battlefield now. SynRgy on one side. Rebels on the other. And the WRAITHs in between, slaughtering everything.

I sprint through the fireline.

Two rebels yell at me to take cover. One recognizes me. Too late.

I grab his rifle mid-hand-off and pivot. Fire into the SynRgy gunners above.

One down. Two. Clip runs dry. I toss the weapon back, sword already moving.

Steel meets flesh meets steel again.

I climb the tower base—hand over hand through burning rebar. WRAITH above me claws at a civilian transport. Pulls it open like a can. Screams spill out.

I don’t stop.

There’s a door at the base of the Stratosphere. Half-blown. I kick it in anyway.

Inside, the light is wrong.

Pale blue. Pulse-beat flicker. Machine-spine architecture.

I move through it like a ghost with teeth.

Two more guards. One sees me. Shouts.

Too slow.

I fire once. Blade twice.

They fall. One twitches. The sword finishes it.

I reach the lift.

The sword hums like it remembers heaven.

I breathe once.

And go down.



####

The top of the Stratosphere is quiet.

Too quiet.

The kind of silence that doesn’t wait for footsteps. It swallows them.

I step out of the lift like stepping into a tomb that forgot what was buried here. Everything gleams. White. Chrome. Impossibly clean. No soot, no bodies, no static screens. Just the whirr of a system still pretending it’s divine.

The carpet is gone. Tile now—marble, maybe. The walls have no seams. The hallway stretches forward like a gullet. I walk it.

Not fast. Not slow.

Just ready.

There are no guards. No checkpoints. No scanners. Just sensor eyes in the walls—dark and quiet, like they’ve already made peace with whatever comes next.

The sword hums steady. Not singing anymore. Just breathing.

I reach a doorway. Marked with a symbol I don’t know. A spiral. Inward. Deep.

It opens when I stand near it. Of course it does.

The chamber beyond is circular. A viewing deck once, maybe. Or an altar.

Screens line the far wall. Not active. Just blank. Waiting.

And in the center—

A lift.

Not up.

Down.

Brushed steel. Ritual seams. Framed like a reliquary.

I step inside. There’s no button. No interface.

The door closes on its own.

The descent begins.

The hum shifts. Lower now. Like something waking up beneath the Earth.

I draw the blade. Just to feel it.

This isn’t ascent.

This is judgment.

And I’m not here for salvation.



####

The lift stops like it’s afraid to open.

Then it does.

The Core isn’t a room. It’s a wound. A chasm wrapped in light. Circular, cathedral-shaped. The walls ripple—liquid metal pulsing with embedded screens. All dark.

Except one.

Her.

Kat. Again. Still. Always. Strapped upright. Broken open. The flame consuming her on a loop, slowed down, frame by frame like it’s holy.

I don’t look away.

He’s standing beneath it. Alone.

Voss.

Not ten feet tall. Not glowing. Not armored.

Just a man in the skin of a prophet.

White suit. Silver trim. Hair slicked back like an ad campaign for obedience. Hands clasped behind his back. Posture like a messiah.

He turns when I step into the chamber. Smiles.

"Ah. There he is. The prodigal son."

I don’t speak.

He walks toward me, slow, like a preacher descending from the pulpit to greet a sinner.

"Do you know how rare it is," he says, "to be genuinely disappointed in someone anymore? Most of the vessels who fail us don’t carry any Spark at all. But you, Lieutenant—no. You were blessed."

He circles me. Not a threat. Just theater.

"I hand-fed you the gospel. I carved discipline into your bones. You walked among angels. Carried a sword forged in revelation. And what did you do?"

I say nothing.

"You fucked a heretic."

He stops in front of me. Not close. Just enough.

"Do you know what it cost us to clean your transgression from the narrative? We had to scrub hours of footage. Rewrite logs. Recycle every Spark that touched her name. And still, the story lingers."

He gestures toward the screen. Her burning.

"That should’ve been the end of it. Correction complete. Error purged. But no. You couldn’t leave it alone."

The sword hums. He hears it. Eyes twitch. Just a little.

"What is it, Lieutenant? You miss the taste of it? The feel? You wanted more? You think that thing loved you?"

My voice finally breaks through.

"Don’t call her that."

He smiles wider.

"You don’t get to dictate the language of God."

The chamber flickers. The walls pulse.

He raises his arms like a showman.

"And now look what you’ve done. You unleashed the vessels. You broke the seals. Do you even know what the WRAITHs were for? They weren’t guards. They were scaffolding. A skeleton for the Protocol to walk in. And now they’re loose."

He steps closer.

"You’ve doomed us all."

I tighten my grip.

"Good."

He chuckles. "Oh, you’re still dramatic. You always were. But you were good, Lieutenant. You had promise. And I don’t believe in waste."

He snaps his fingers. A hidden panel opens. A display. A contract, glowing faint blue.

"You can still come back. You’re valuable. The Protocol forgives. I forgive. Join me, and you’ll be part of the final schema. You’ll be a template. We’ll model entire fleets after you."

I stare at the document. Its name reads: PATTERN: BLAKE.01.

He watches me.

"You want Kat back? You want her Spark? We can give you that. Not the broken flesh. But the Signal. The data. You could talk to her again. Hear her voice. We’d even give her a new body. A perfect one."

My voice is a whisper.

"Can I have her Spark?"

He smiles.

"Of course not."

The blade enters his chest before he finishes the laugh.

He stares at it. At me.

"You—"

I twist.

"Wrong answer."

He falls. Hard. Arms out like a crucifixion.

The floor beneath him cracks. A glow leaks out—sick and gold and screaming.

The chamber shudders.

The walls flicker, screens blink alive. Every face I’ve ever killed stares back.

And then the Core begins to fold.

Not explode.

Fold.

Inward. Like a scar stitching shut.

The hum rises. The walls convulse. A pressure like gravity weeps from the breach.

I back away, blade drawn. But nothing attacks.

Not yet.

His body sinks into the floor.

The last thing I see is his hand—twitching, pointing toward the flame.

Then he’s gone.

I’m alone.

But alive.

And the world?

It’s ending. Just like she said it would.
